capital_efficiency.label: protect capital
capital_efficiency.summary: Efficient capital use can mean not adding risk until the shock is re-checked.
capital_efficiency.do_nothing_risk: Doing nothing preserves optionality, but if the shock is real it can leave exposed capital unprotected or new buys mistimed.
capital_efficiency.priority_reason: Protection and timing risk outrank new adds while the shock is unresolved.
capital_efficiency.timing_balance: Avoid waiting for a perfect bottom; if live checks confirm, consider staged exposure rather than all-or-nothing timing.
compare_against: risk reduction or hedging if event risk is active

what_changed: Action remains high-impact with fresh enough evidence; still run the gate.
invalidates_if: Fresh price, flow, position, source, or event-risk evidence changes the expected risk/reward.
assumption_refresh.next_step: Keep loud, but confirm same-session gate before any capital move.
assumption_refresh.honesty_rule: Assumption refresh can downgrade stale or missing evidence; it does not execute or auto-promote trades.

actions.what: Event risk: Middle East oil/rates shock can affect new-buy timing
actions.your_move: Review exposure, hedges, and new buys before acting today: Middle East oil/rates shock can affect new-buy timing (oil, rates, volatility, energy, growth). If the event changes oil/rates/vol or a held sleeve, decide whether to hold, hedge, trim, or wait.
actions.why: Fundstrat technical work flagged a near-term WTI and 10-year yield bounce tied to Gulf/Iran-war headlines before a possible reversal; review exposure before new risk adds.
actions.why_it_moves_goal: Fast exogenous shocks can change sizing, hedging, and opportunity cost before normal source lanes update.
missing_evidence: WTI approaches 99-101, 10Y yield approaches roughly 4.55-4.59, or Strait/ceasefire headlines change abruptly.

actions.what: Event risk: Iran closure threat re-escalates Hormuz oil/rates watch
actions.your_move: Review exposure, hedges, and new buys before acting today: Iran closure threat re-escalates Hormuz oil/rates watch (oil, rates, volatility, energy, growth, shipping). If the event changes oil/rates/vol or a held sleeve, decide whether to hold, hedge, trim, or wait.
actions.why: Iran said it was closing the Strait of Hormuz after renewed Lebanon strikes, while CENTCOM disputed actual closure and public prediction markets still show partial-but-not-normal traffic recovery. Decision impact: keep the oil/rates event-risk lane high and keep Monday new-buy timing gated on WTI/TNX/shipping confirmation rather than clearing the shock.
actions.why_it_moves_goal: Fast exogenous shocks can change sizing, hedging, and opportunity cost before normal source lanes update.
missing_evidence: Disconfirm if IMF PortWatch finalized calls, shipping/insurance reports, WTI, and TNX show sustained normalization; re-escalate if traffic drops again, toll/insurance rules bite, or US-Iran talks fail.

actions.what: Event risk: Fundstrat flags mixed Iran/Hormuz peace-flow risk

actions.why: Progress toward a U.S.-Iran peace path is market-positive through lower oil/yields, but contradictory Strait of Hormuz reports keep oil/rates shock risk active.
actions.why_it_moves_goal: Fast exogenous shocks can change sizing, hedging, and opportunity cost before normal source lanes update.
missing_evidence: Recheck Hormuz flow, oil, yields, and semis before promoting adds or reducing hedges.

capital_efficiency.label: protect capital
capital_efficiency.summary: Efficient capital use can mean not adding risk until the shock is re-checked.
capital_efficiency.do_nothing_risk: Doing nothing preserves optionality, but if the shock is real it can leave exposed capital unprotected or new buys mistimed.
capital_efficiency.priority_reason: Protection and timing risk outrank new adds while the shock is unresolved.
capital_efficiency.timing_balance: Avoid waiting for a perfect bottom; if live checks confirm, consider staged exposure rather than all-or-nothing timing.

radar.author: Newton
radar.quote: Break below 74.40 keeps the path of least resistance lower toward 71.

radar.author: Newton
radar.quote: Corrective bounce toward 4.554-4.586% should precede a turn lower if the ceasefire path holds.

radar.author: Newton
radar.quote: Bounce only; resistance near 175.72 should repel price toward 162.

radar.author: Fundstrat
radar.quote: GOOGL is attractive technically on weakness, with support from 341 to 350 over the next week; the capital raise should be only a temporary negative.

radar.author: Newton
radar.quote: June 5 technical note makes 695-705 the first support check; failure shifts risk toward a 675 second-leg target. Re-check Monday pre-market before adding AI beta.

radar.author: Newton
radar.quote: June 5 read favors equal-weighted/value/cyclical participation over tech-heavy cap-weighted exposure while growth consolidates. Use as rotation context, not an auto-trade.

radar.author: Newton
radar.quote: SOX held near 12,114 base-line support after the June 5 break; a daily close below it would raise tech-underperformance risk. Re-check before semi adds.

radar.author: Farrell
radar.quote: Near-term patience remains preferred: reserve rebuild should ease immediate bearish pressure, but reserve coverage near 7 months still argues against adding until STRC demand improves.

radar.author: Newton
radar.quote: Newton expects Gold to finish its decline soon, with the $4,100-$4,250 zone the area to watch for adding precious-metals exposure as yields and the Dollar roll over.

radar.author: Newton
radar.quote: Equal-weight Technology broke its uptrend after a steep overbought run; bearish engulfing plus trend break should allow Tech weakness and supports Growth-to-Value rotation.

radar.author: Newton
radar.quote: Broader market is not too bearish outside Tech; watch whether weakness joins QQQ/SPX or holds up. Crude break positive, but risk-asset benefit is doubtful while Tech falls.

radar.author: Newton
radar.quote: EWY looks vulnerable into next week toward 167, with heavier pressure opening 151-154; treat it as a semiconductor-growth read-through and avoid chasing tech beta on this weakness.

radar.author: Newton
radar.quote: XHS cleared the 2021 highs near 116 and remains a favored long into summer seasonality; use dips back to 116-118 to add, with 132 as the upside objective over time.

radar.author: Mark L. Newton, CMT
radar.quote: Newton says a confirmed WTI break below 85.95 would trigger the larger bearish breakdown and can open downside toward 60 into September if the ceasefire path holds.

radar.author: Mark L. Newton, CMT
radar.quote: Newton said REITs are breaking out on above-average volume and he is overweight the space; EWRE is pushing to new highs and he expects REIT outperformance if rates fall on an Iran deal.

radar.author: Mark L. Newton, CMT
radar.quote: Newton says US equity lows likely are in place; use pullbacks to buy dips as breadth broadens and crude/TNX weaken. Near-term SPX resistance is 7456-7475, support near 7360, with some backing and filling possible into early next week.

radar.author: Sean Farrell
radar.quote: Farrell says several bearish crypto narratives eased, but BTC/ETH remain rangebound and crypto has not attracted incremental demand; stance should remain patient ahead of FOMC.

radar.author: Fundstrat Direct / Mark Newton
radar.quote: Fundstrat notes SpaceX IPO cash-raising likely pressured equities; Newton says do not chase the initial pop and expects a chance to buy between now and October.

radar.author: Mark L. Newton, CMT
radar.quote: Equal-weight Industrials broke above multi-month resistance near 62 and can test 64, but relative confirmation versus equal-weight S&P is still needed.

radar.author: Mark L. Newton, CMT
radar.quote: Energy remains a weak spot as WTI breaks down; avoid buying dips in RYE/Energy until crude stabilizes and relative trends repair.

radar.author: Mark L. Newton, CMT
radar.quote: Newton says equal-weight Industrials and Utilities have broken out, with XLI confirming a genuine upside breakout and sector broadening beyond Tech over the next 1-2 months.

radar.author: Sean Farrell
radar.quote: HYPE reclaimed crypto leadership after improved incentive and market-structure signals; promote it to research/watch priority, not an automatic buy.

radar.author: Sean Farrell
radar.quote: Strategy stabilization is constructive, but STRC reserve coverage remains below the confidence threshold; keep MSTR/STRC funding flywheel on risk watch.

radar.author: Mark L. Newton, CMT
radar.quote: Newton says Utilities have broken above a multi-month downtrend; RSPU and XLU are up about 1% and the February pattern now points to potential new highs for XLU in the months ahead.

radar.author: Alexa Sinsheimer and Max Motz
radar.quote: Fed Watch says Warsh removed forward guidance and preserved optionality; do not read the higher dot median as a firm hike path until the new framework/task-force outputs clarify.

radar.author: Mark L. Newton, CMT
radar.quote: Newton flags Financials/banks as risk-on leadership; UBS has broken out from a 19-year base and is one of his favorites, with expected push over $60 over the next month.

radar.author: Mark L. Newton, CMT
radar.quote: Newton flags Biotech outperformance inside Healthcare; XBI is breaking out to a five-year high while IBB lags, with upside target near $173 around the Feb 2021 peak.

radar.author: Mark L. Newton, CMT
radar.quote: Newton calls DAL attractive airline leadership alongside JETS, tied to falling crude and a potential intermediate-term airline breakout.

radar.author: Mark L. Newton, CMT
radar.window: weeks ahead / late next week oil tailwind
radar.quote: Newton likes long JETS as airlines near a multi-year breakout; weekly close above 32 confirms, WTI mid-60s pullback could make breakout imminent.

radar.author: Mark L. Newton, CMT
radar.quote: Dollar risk should be re-checked: Newton flags USDJPY reversal risk and expects Yen strength / weaker USD if Strait reopening outweighs Warsh hawkishness.

radar.author: Mark L. Newton, CMT
radar.quote: XHB reached Newton???s initial 112 target; he expects work toward the 123 range top as lower 10-year yields support homebuilders.

radar.author: Mark L. Newton, CMT
radar.window: near-term into early July; Q3 risk
radar.quote: Newton says ETH/BTC have started near-term bounces from June lows; watch ETH $1987-$2013 and BTC $70k-$71k resistance, then Q3 pullback/retest risk.

radar.author: Mark L. Newton, CMT
radar.quote: Daily Technical says MSFT is testing intermediate support; stabilization is likely, but failure below 355 invites more selling.

radar.author: Kent Fung
radar.quote: Detail page frames AMD's $10B Taiwan investment as supply-chain bottleneck preparation; watch whether this strengthens AMD AI execution/research priority vs the existing top-prospect thesis.

radar.author: Mark L. Newton, CMT
radar.quote: Newton flags silver's 5% drop as a likely final-leg decline; watch for a brief undercut of June lows/front-month silver below 60, then a rally attempt over the next couple weeks.

Add context to rationale drawers without using it as standalone tactical proof.

rows.label: Pre-market crash triage
rows.why: High-volatility or re-check posture needs broad tape triage before single-name action.
rows.operator_question: Do we need to cut exposure, hedge, wait, or prepare add-on entries after forced selling?
rows.freshness_requirement: Market/flow lanes should be same-session; Friday evidence is stale by Monday pre-market.

tactical_top3.why: Strategic upgrade plus strongest short-term momentum; up 7.1% over the prior 30 days, Trend Score moved from #10 to #3, and seasonality supports June-August relative strength.
Carry-over top-3 sector and largest benchmark overweight; Trend Score #1 and broad member participation support adding on pullbacks ahead of a REIT breakout.
Expected to outperform as yields fall, with crude and long-term yields late in their bounces and ceasefire progress as a possible catalyst.

tactical_bottom3.why: Third consecutive tactical bottom-3 month; Newton overrides strong backward-looking quant ranks because a more concrete ceasefire and Strait reopening could flush crude lower.
New bottom-3 sector after the weakest May-to-June performance in coverage; travel/services demand and EPS revisions remain soft despite an XHB sub-sector offset.
Defensive laggard with weaker risk/reward than Health Care, Utilities, or REITs; remains the largest benchmark underweight.

named_levels.why: A weekly close above the $38 area would argue for an upside resolution of the multi-year symmetrical triangle.

monthly_checklist.decision_use: Use tactical 3x3 as timing color through the next monthly sector allocation update, then supersede it.
Lean Health Care, Real Estate, and Utilities over Energy, Consumer Discretionary, and Staples when comparing sector ETF adds.
Treat Tech as trimmed from double-overweight, not a bearish thesis break; stretched names need base-building before full-size adds.
Weekly close above $38 confirms the REIT breakout layer and can upgrade REIT/Real Estate timing.
CIBR, IHF, ARKG, IYT, and DRIV are the current 3% tactical ETF basket names; deleted May ETFs should not be treated as active Fundstrat adds.

options_expression.line: 3 options idea(s) ready to act now; 9 names checked. these ideas use ~3.5% of your ~10% options budget.

rows.action: Buy 14x NVDA 2026-08-21 $205 calls (~$17.50 each) — most you can lose: $24,500 (1.3% of book) — scalable to 21 at your full options cap.
rows.reason: You already have conviction on NVDA; this expresses the upside with defined, capped risk. Options are relatively cheap here (low IV rank), so buying premium is more defensible.
rows.evidence: Long options lose value as time passes; a 100% loss is on the table, so keep the size small.
rows.the_catch: Long options lose value as time passes; a 100% loss is on the table, so keep the size small.

glossary.premium: What you pay for the option. For a long option this is also the most you can lose.
glossary.max loss: The worst case in real dollars (and % of your portfolio) if it goes to zero. For these, plan on it as a real outcome.
glossary.defined risk: The loss is capped and known up front — you can't lose more than the premium you put in.
glossary.IV rank: How expensive options are right now versus this name's own past year — 0 = cheapest, 100 = most expensive.
glossary.expected move: How far the options market is pricing the stock to move by expiry. Your idea needs more than this to pay off.
glossary.break-even: Where the stock has to be at expiry for you to make money back on the option.
glossary.delta: Roughly how stock-like the option is, and a rough chance it finishes in-the-money. ~0.70 acts a lot like the stock; ~0.30 is cheaper but more of a long shot.

rows.honesty: A 100% loss of the premium is a realistic outcome — this is sized for that.

rows.action: Buy 11x SMH 2026-08-21 $650/$700 call debit spread (~$22.79 net) — most you can lose: $25,069 (1.3% of book) — scalable to 16 at your full options cap.
rows.reason: You already have conviction on SMH; this expresses the upside with defined, capped risk.
rows.evidence: Options are expensive right now (high IV rank) — the spread keeps you from overpaying for that.

glossary.IV rank: How expensive options are right now versus this name's own past year — 0 = cheapest, 100 = most expensive.
glossary.debit spread: Buy one option and sell a further-out one against it. Costs less, caps both your risk and your upside, and sells off some of the expensive IV.

_doc.what_this_is: Operator-tunable sizing dials. These are the knobs that decide the LIVE suggested position size on a BUY/ADD card. Conviction drives the size UP; every former hard cap is a dial here, defaulted OFF or generous. Nothing here auto-executes a trade -- the dashboard only SUGGESTS a size; the operator places every order by hand.
how_the_size_is_computed_plain_language: 1. Start from the proposed dollars the reallocation brief already put on the card (the existing anchor). If that is zero, fall back to base_size_usd.

2. Conviction LIFT. Only an HONEST, high-conviction, non-conflicted, BUY-aligned read lifts the size. The lift multiplier is: 1 + conviction_size_slope * conviction_strength, where conviction_strength is a 0..1 number built from the read band (HIGH=1.0, MODERATE=0.5, LOW/none=0.0), the 1..5 strength rung (scaled to 0..1), and whether the evidence converges across at least min_converging_groups independent groups. A name with no read, a conflicted read, a SELL/NEUTRAL read, or a single-group read gets NO lift (multiplier 1.0). This is the F1 honesty rail: size keys off the independence-collapsed, freshness-decayed conviction, never off raw echo or stale counts.

3. SOFT MAX dials (optional, default OFF). per_name_soft_max_usd and concentration_soft_max_pct are NOT hard clamps. When set and the lifted size exceeds them, the size is held at the soft max AND a visible note states the original lifted number and which dial bit. When null, they do nothing -- the conviction-driven size flows through unclamped.

4. CASH REALITY (informational, never a block). If a real available-cash / buying-power number exists in the account data, the card shows it and flags exceeds_cash when the suggested size is larger -- as a NUMBER, never a hidden block. When cash data is absent it shows not_checked. Nothing is silently reduced.

5. FUNDING REALITY (informational, never a block). The operator rarely holds cash; a buy is almost always funded by SELLING something else. So the honest affordability number is cash on hand PLUS the sellable funding pool from the reallocation brief (feed.reallocation_brief.funding.pool_total_usd -- already sell-gate-ordered: AI-factor wrappers first, broad reservoirs last, funding-protected wrappers excluded). When treat_funding_pool_as_available is true (default) the card adds the pool to cash to get funding_available_usd, and shows exceeds_funding / funding_shortfall_usd (max(0, suggested - funding_available)) plus a plain-language funding_note. This NEVER changes suggested_usd or the conviction lift -- it only ADDS the affordability number; the human decides.

6. ROUND (display precision, FINAL step). Round EVERY dollar amount the card shows -- suggested_usd plus the cash/funding fields available_cash, funding_pool_usd, funding_available_usd, funding_shortfall_usd, and the cap_room context dollar -- to the NEAREST size_rounding_usd (default $100), and store it as a WHOLE NUMBER so no decimal tail ever renders -- on the static card AND in the live client-side recompute when the operator drags the adjustable sizing bar (the render mirrors this exact formula, so it rounds identically). The covered/short booleans (exceeds_cash / exceeds_funding) are recomputed from the ROUNDED numbers so the displayed dollars and the verdict agree. Multipliers and percentages (size_lift_mult, size_lift_strength, ceiling_pct) are NOT dollars and are left untouched. This is display precision ONLY: it does NOT change the conviction lift, eligibility, the funding logic, or which names size up.

Former HARD caps now removed as limits and represented here as dials: the static TIER_BANDS ceiling, the concentration cap, the cap_room clamp, and the sell-gate-as-blocker are no longer hard stops. tier_ceiling_is_soft_reference=true means the tier ceiling is shown for context only and does NOT clip the size.
_doc.size_rounding_usd_doc: Display-precision dial for the FINAL step of the sizing formula. Every dollar amount the card shows is rounded to the nearest size_rounding_usd and stored as a whole number (int) so no long decimal tail ever renders -- the operator did not want decimals on the sizing numbers when dragging the adjustable size bar. Set 1 for whole dollars, 100 for the nearest hundred (default), or 1000 for the nearest thousand. A 0/None/negative value disables step rounding but the amount is still rounded to a whole dollar so no decimal ever shows. Round-to-nearest, e.g. 151890.48 -> 151900. This is display precision ONLY: it never changes the conviction lift, eligibility, the funding logic, or which names size up.
_doc.treat_funding_pool_as_available_doc: When true (default), the funding-aware affordability reality counts cash on hand PLUS the reallocation-brief sellable funding pool (pool_total_usd) as what could fund a suggested buy -- because the operator usually funds a buy by CONVERTING one ETF/stock to another, not from idle cash. When false, only literal cash on hand counts (funding_available_usd == cash). Either way it is informational only: never blocks, never clamps, never changes suggested_usd. The funding pool is REUSED from the reallocation brief, which is already sell-gate-ordered and excludes funding-protected / live-thesis-at-its-low names -- the sell-gate is NOT re-implemented here.
_doc.evidence_rail_note: This file tunes SIZING only. It contains no rail key that tunables.py forbids (those rails are non-tunable and live in tunables.py). A genuinely CONFLICTED card is still routed to RESOLVE/RECHECK and is never auto-sized-up -- that is enforced in the engine, not a dial you can turn off here.

ideas.move: Buy 14x NVDA 2026-08-21 $205 calls (~$17.50 each) — most you can lose: $24,500 (1.3% of book) — scalable to 21 at your full options cap.
ideas.why: You already have conviction on NVDA; this expresses the upside with defined, capped risk. Options are relatively cheap here (low IV rank), so buying premium is more defensible.
ideas.the_catch: Long options lose value as time passes; a 100% loss is on the table, so keep the size small.

glossary.premium: What you pay for the option. For a long option this is also the most you can lose.
glossary.max loss: The worst case in real dollars (and % of your portfolio) if it goes to zero. For these, plan on it as a real outcome.
glossary.defined risk: The loss is capped and known up front — you can't lose more than the premium you put in.
glossary.IV rank: How expensive options are right now versus this name's own past year — 0 = cheapest, 100 = most expensive.
glossary.expected move: How far the options market is pricing the stock to move by expiry. Your idea needs more than this to pay off.
glossary.break-even: Where the stock has to be at expiry for you to make money back on the option.
glossary.delta: Roughly how stock-like the option is, and a rough chance it finishes in-the-money. ~0.70 acts a lot like the stock; ~0.30 is cheaper but more of a long shot.

ideas.honesty: A 100% loss of the premium is a realistic outcome — this is sized for that.

glossary.premium: What you pay for the option. For a long option this is also the most you can lose.
glossary.max loss: The worst case in real dollars (and % of your portfolio) if it goes to zero. For these, plan on it as a real outcome.
glossary.defined risk: The loss is capped and known up front — you can't lose more than the premium you put in.
glossary.IV rank: How expensive options are right now versus this name's own past year — 0 = cheapest, 100 = most expensive.
glossary.debit spread: Buy one option and sell a further-out one against it. Costs less, caps both your risk and your upside, and sells off some of the expensive IV.
glossary.expected move: How far the options market is pricing the stock to move by expiry. Your idea needs more than this to pay off.
glossary.break-even: Where the stock has to be at expiry for you to make money back on the option.
glossary.delta: Roughly how stock-like the option is, and a rough chance it finishes in-the-money. ~0.70 acts a lot like the stock; ~0.30 is cheaper but more of a long shot.
